claim: A nāga-king (Kāḷa) has a subterranean palace/realm (bhavana) beneath the water.
Mucalinda the nāga-king can magically create a divine mansion, showing iddhi power.

note: devas-men-nāgas in a list of rejoicing beings; passing mention
nāgas in a list of beings (gandhabbas, supaṇṇas, kinnaras) who behold the Buddha
nāga-supaṇṇa-yakkha in a list of beings who rejoice
men-nāgas-gandhabbas in a list; passing mention
crowd wonders if it is a nāga-possession vs bhūta/yakkha/devatā; speculation, no assertion

claim: Sixteen thousand nāga-maidens attend the nāga-prince; depicts the nāga realm's society.
note: bodhisatta context but claim is about nāga-maiden retinue

claim: Virūpakkha is named overlord-great-king of the nāgas (nāgādhipati).
note: identifies Virūpakkha as ruler of the nāga class

claim: The nāga realm (nāgabhavana) is entered by sinking into the earth/water; nāga-youths serve there.
Nāga-maidens dive into the earth and return to the nāga realm; they fear the supaṇṇa.
note: realm access + supaṇṇa-fear
narrative: showing/handing over the nāga at the uposatha-spot; no ontological assertion

claim: The captured nāga sinks into the earth and returns to the nāga realm.
note: earth-entry to nāgabhavana
narrative: the great nāga is boxed and made to dance; mahānāga as address; no assertion
narrative: snake-charmer feeds and displays the nāga-king; passing
gloss identifying which nāga is meant; locative description, no ontological claim

claim: Deities wish to hear Dhamma from Bhūridatta the nāga; implies a nāga can teach/that beings seek dhamma via him.
note: borderline; the dhamma-hearing motive re a nāga is asserted
narrative contest between charmer and nāga; no assertion

claim: The nāga-king's venom/heat scorches the captor into a leper; then he creates an adorned form (shapeshift).
note: venom-power + form-creation
narrative: returns to the nāga realm after teaching dhamma to uncle; passing
section heading only (Campeyyanāga-cariyā-vaṇṇanā)

claim: The bodhisatta was born as the nāga-king Campeyyaka in the nāga realm beneath the Campā river.
note: birth as nāga + realm location

claim: The nāga-king keeps the cātuddasī uposatha, leaving the nāga realm to lie on an anthill.
note: uposatha-keeping by a nāga
narrative: man learns spell and approaches to capture the nāga; passing

claim: The nāga endures torture without breaking, guarding his virtue (sīla) during the uposatha vow.
note: sīla-keeping nāga; moral capacity
narrative: nāga-king refuses to eat for fear of being killed; passing
narrative: nāga-king to dance in the royal courtyard; passing

claim: As nāga-king Campeyyaka he was dhamma-faring and observed the eightfold ariya uposatha.
note: dhammacārī nāga keeping uposatha
narrative: nāga-queen Sumanā leaves the realm to search for her husband; passing

claim: The virtuous dhamma-faring nāga-king keeps cātuddasī/pannarasī uposatha, has iddhi power, and a deva-like retinue.
note: uposatha + power + realm-splendour; uposatha primary
verse: the freed nāga addresses the king; narrative speech, no assertion

claim: The nāga keeps fortnightly uposatha and his merit fills the heavenly city; depicts a nāga's meritorious practice.
note: uposatha-practice and its fruit

claim: Campeyyaka the nāga-king was the Bodhisatta (Lokanātha).
note: jātaka identification: bodhisatta as nāga
cross-reference list of cariyās (hatthināga, Bhūridatta, Campeyya); name-in-list

claim: As nāga-king Saṅkhapāla he kept residence by way of the uposatha-dwelling.
note: uposatha-vāsa by a nāga

claim: The bodhisatta, wandering through births, was reborn in the nāga realm as Saṅkhapāla; the uposatha cannot be kept there so he leaves, longing for a human birth.
note: birth-mechanics + nāga realm as path-impediment for uposatha

claim: Hunters resolve to kill and eat the nāga-king (as game meat).
note: nāga as predation target by humans
narrative: man bids the wounded nāga go free; mahānāga as address; passing

claim: The nāga realm has splendour, maidens, and divine pleasures; a human guest dwells there a year then ordains.
note: realm-description + human visiting nāgabhavana
verse: 'having heard the nāga' — quoted gāthā, passing
verse variant: 'having heard the nāga'; passing

claim: Saṅkhapāla the nāga-king was the Bodhisatta (Lokanātha).
note: jātaka identification: bodhisatta as nāga

claim: Leaving the nāga realm's deva-like wealth to do the ascetic's duty exemplifies the renunciation perfection.
note: nāga-birth wealth renounced; pāramī framing implies realm-status
a hunter wonders whether the figure is a deva or a nāga; speculation, no assertion about nāgas
cross-reference list of jātaka episodes (Campeyya/Sīlava nāga-king times); names in list

claim: An aspiration to Buddhahood succeeds only from a human birth, not for one in the nāga/supaṇṇa class.
note: path-ceiling: nāga cannot fulfil the Buddha-aspiration

claim: Nāgas are listed among dangers (with yakkhas, rakkhasas, pisācas) from which the gift of safety protects.
note: nāga framed as a source of danger/harm to beings
deva-nāga-householders in a list of those who honoured Mahāgovinda; passing
section heading only (Sīlava-nāgarāja-jātaka-vaṇṇanā)
nāgarāja here = bull-elephant (asīti-sahassa-vāraṇa retinue); narrative naming, no claim
jātaka identification of Sīlava-nāgarāja (elephant context); name-in-samodhāna, no serpent ontology
uddāna mnemonic list of jātaka names (Sīlava-nāga etc.); name-in-list
narrative: news that Sāgata tamed the nāga spread; passing report of an event

claim: The Ambatittha nāga-king (whom Sāgata tamed) is contrasted as far more formidable than an ordinary water-snake.
note: asserts the nāga's potency relative to a mere snake
verse asking in which direction the nāga-king stands; quoted gāthā, narrative question
verse addressing 'nāga' (taunt about fearing wind/wood); narrative speech

claim: In the jātaka identification the nāga was a monk and the bodhisatta was the tree-deity.
note: samodhāna: nāga = a being reborn as a monk; identity assertion

claim: A fierce nāga-king resolves to raise fire from the water and burn the tree to drive off the birds.
note: weather/fire power of a nāga
verse: men-nāgas-gandhabbas in a list paying homage; passing
crowd speculates nāga-possession vs bhūta/yakkha/devatā; speculation duplicate of p507

claim: The bodhisatta, a mighty nāga-king named Atula, gave a golden seat to the Buddha and was prophesied to future Buddhahood.
note: bodhisatta-as-nāga + iddhi-might
cross-reference list of jātaka times (Campeyya/Bhūridatta/Chaddanta nāga-king); names in list
nāgas-supaṇṇas among devatās honouring with scents; list/scenery
devas-nāgas-supaṇṇas honour from the sky with garlands; list/scenery

claim: A nāga, if it is one, sinks into the earth and travels (vs deva goes by air, human eats alms); a diagnostic of nāga nature.
note: earth-entry as defining nāga behaviour

claim: The nāga-king Kāḷa dwells in a subterranean realm of vast extent; the golden bowl sinks to his palace.
note: nāga realm/abode beneath the river
nāga-yakkha-supaṇṇa honour the bodhisatta with scents; list/scenery

claim: The great nāga-king Mahākāḷa flees and sinks into the earth to the Mañjerika nāga realm.
note: named nāga realm + earth-entry
nāgas to nāgas, supaṇṇas to supaṇṇas proclaim victory; list/scenery
verse: the host of nāgas proclaimed victory; passing/scenery

claim: Mucalinda the nāga-king encircled the Buddha sevenfold with his coils to ward off cold and rain.
note: protective coiling; bodily power of the nāga

claim: The nāga-king Maṇikaṇṭha leaves the nāga realm and wanders in the guise of a brahmin youth.
note: shapeshift to human form

claim: The nāga wears a wish-granting jewel and embraces with his coils; advice exploits his coil-behaviour.
note: jewel-bearing + coil behaviour of nāga
narrative: nāga repeatedly flees the begging ascetic; speaks two verses; passing action
narrative: nāga-king dives and returns to his realm, no longer comes back; passing
verse: the nāga, begged for his jewel, vanished; quoted gāthā summarising story

claim: In the jātaka identification the nāga-king was Sāriputta (and the caravan-leader the bodhisatta).
note: samodhāna identity: nāga = Sāriputta
uddāna mnemonic list including 'nāga'; name-in-list
narrative: a nāga-king sees and questions; sets up a riddle; passing

claim: Quarrelling nāga-kings make the water turbid; their concord clears it — nāgas affect the water.
note: nāga influence over water (weather/water power)
narrative: message conveyed to the nāga-king among others; passing list of recipients

claim: Former relatives, through greed for the palace, were reborn as fish, turtles, nāgas, and frogs.
note: karma_cause: greed → rebirth as nāga (among aquatic animals)

claim: In the jātaka identification the nāga and the supaṇṇa were two ministers; the ascetic the bodhisatta.
note: samodhāna identity: nāga = a being reborn as a minister
simile: 'like an adorned nāga-prince' (alaṅkata-nāga-kumāro viya); simile vehicle

claim: A nāga-king reborn on the island magically makes his own body into a great ship to rescue.
note: shapeshift/iddhi: nāga creates a ship-body
narrative: sea-deity takes the nāga-king to her mansion; passing

claim: In the jātaka identification the nāga-king was Sāriputta (the sea-deity the bodhisatta).
note: samodhāna identity: nāga = Sāriputta
place-name Nāgadīpa (an island), geography; scenery

claim: Names the four nāga-king royal lineages (Virūpakkha, Erāpatha, Chabyāputta, Kaṇhāgotamaka).
note: classification of nāga-rāja families (paritta context)

claim: Cultivating mettā toward the four nāga-king families ensures snakes (dīghajātika) will not bite or harm.
note: four nāga families + apotropaic relation; classification primary
yakkha-nāga offerings (balikamma) mentioned amid a famine; passing cultic mention

claim: A nāga-king leaves his realm to forage on land, is mistaken for a snake; later enters the king's chamber bearing jewels.
note: land-foraging nāga + realm; mistaken-for-snake; jewel-bearing

claim: A nāga-maiden abandons her form to consort with a water-snake; nāga-youths kill by deadly breath-wind.
note: shapeshift (abandons form) + venomous breath power

claim: A Ganges nāga-king, angered, makes a huge body and rears his hood (phaṇa) to drown people.
note: size-change + hood + water power

claim: Wise beings of old, though established in nāga-king status, dwelt three years in filth through the fault of anger.
note: karma_cause: anger's fruit even within nāga existence

claim: The bodhisatta was born in the Daddara nāga realm at the foot of the Himalayas; a nāga-king rules there over nāga-youths.
note: named nāga realm + its kingship
many nāgas and supaṇṇas came to watch a festival; list/scenery
verse: nāga-king threatens harm for the killing of his young; narrative speech
place-name Nāgadīpa (island geography); scenery (supaṇṇa-birth context)
place-name Nāgadīpa (island geography); scenery
verse mentioning feeding a nāga-king with a lump; here likely the bull-elephant in battle context (kavaca, rājakumāro); passing
gloss of the war-elephant verse (nāgarāja = the mother-supporting tusker ridden in battle); elephant sense, passing
question 'who are you among nāgas, yakkhas, supaṇṇas, Sakka...'; speculation list

claim: The Buddha works manifold benefit even for nāgas and supaṇṇas (alongside giving humans refuges, precepts, path-fruits).
note: nāgas as recipients of the Buddha's benefiting activity; hears_dhamma/benefit

claim: A nāga-king takes a man into the nāga realm, shares his splendour for a year, then returns him to the human path.
note: realm-visit + nāga hospitality

claim: By the power of the deity and the nāga-king the man was taken to and from the nāga realm.
note: nāga realm visit recounted (parallel to p264)
king asks whether a nāga/supaṇṇa/deva gives the mangoes; speculation list, passing
place-name Nāgadīpa (island geography); scenery

claim: The nāga-king recalls granting drink, divine food, attendants, and cartloads of jewels to men.
note: nāga as bestower of divine wealth/boons (iddhi-provision)
verse spoken by the nāga-king ordering destruction; attribution note, narrative speech

claim: In the jātaka identification the nāga-king was Sāriputta (the caravan-leader the bodhisatta).
note: samodhāna identity: nāga = Sāriputta

claim: The bodhisatta, coveting a nāga-king's splendour, was reborn in his palace as a nāga and lamented falling into the animal womb.
note: karma_cause: craving nāga-glory → rebirth as nāga (tiracchānayoni)

claim: The nāga keeps cātuddasī uposatha, leaving the nāga realm to lie on an anthill.
note: uposatha-keeping (Campeyya parallel)

claim: The nāga endures torture without breaking, for fear of breaking his sīla during the vow.
note: sīla-keeping nāga under torture (Campeyya parallel)
narrative: nāga-queen leaves the realm to search for her husband; passing (parallel)

claim: She declares 'I am not a goddess, gandhabbī, or human — I am a nāga-maiden.'
note: explicit self-classification as nāga-kaññā distinct from devī/human

claim: Gloss: the nāga-king kept the five precepts and the uposatha-dwelling as weighty.
note: uposatha/sīla observance by nāga

claim: Verse: the nāga-king observes the fourteenth and fifteenth (uposatha) days at the crossroads.
note: uposatha-keeping (quoted gāthā but asserts the practice)

claim: Gloss: the virtuous dhamma-faring nāga-king lies keeping the fourfold-resolve uposatha vow.
note: uposatha-keeping gloss

claim: The nāga-king leaves the box, sheds that form, and takes the form of an adorned brahmin youth.
note: explicit shapeshift to human form
verse: the freed nāga addresses the king; narrative speech (parallel to p018)

claim: Gloss: the nāga's dwelling is the lovely Campeyya nāga realm he invites the king to see.

verse: the king is asked to show compassion to the nāga-clan; narrative speech
narrative: king readies his army to go to the nāga realm; passing
verse: yoke the elephants, let us see the nāga's dwellings; narrative speech
narrative: king proceeds resplendent toward the nāga realm; passing

claim: By his own power the nāga makes his realm all jewel-built with walls and gate-towers and an adorned road.
note: iddhi: magical fashioning of the nāga realm
verse: nāga-maidens danced in Campeyya's dwelling; scenery
verse: the king ascended amid the nāga-maidens' dance; scenery

claim: The king asks why a nāga with such splendour leaves it to keep uposatha on an anthill in the human world.
note: contrast of nāga-realm splendour vs human world; sets up the realm-comparison
gloss describing sixteen thousand nāga-maidens' adornments; scenery/retinue detail

claim: Verse: 'You, mighty nāga-king possessed of all sense-pleasures — is the human world better than this?'
note: explicit comparison: is the human realm superior to the nāga realm
narrative: the nāga-king speaks in reply; passing speech-frame
verse: 'having seen the women and you, nāga, I will do much merit'; narrative speech
gloss of the preceding verse (your nāga-maidens); passing
narrative: then the nāga-king said; speech-frame
narrative: the king takes leave of the nāga-king; passing
narrative: nāga-king has drums beaten in the realm to give away wealth; passing
uddāna mnemonic list including 'Paṇḍara-nāga-vara'; name-in-list
verse asking in which direction the nāga-king stands (Chaddanta riddle); quoted gāthā

verse: 'there the nāga-king bathes' (pond description); scenery
verse: he saw the pond where the nāga-king bathes; scenery

claim: Verse: 'she was then the princess; I was then the nāga-king' — the bodhisatta identifies as the nāga.
note: bodhisatta-as-nāga (Chaddanta/Paṇḍara identification verse)

claim: Verse: 'I was there then, monks; I was then the nāga-king' — the bodhisatta was the nāga.

claim: Gloss: the bodhisatta was the nāga-king pierced by the poisoned arrow; the princess was the instigator, Devadatta the hunter.

claim: Gloss: 'I was none other than the nāga-king there at Chaddanta lake.'
